docs.title: Welcome to SIGGD Docs
docs.text:
Purdue's Special Interest Group in Game Development

We make games. We learn together. We ship things.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Tutorials & Guides

    Step-by-step tutorials covering programming, art, design, audio, and general game-dev workflow — for all skill levels.

    Browse tutorials

  • Projects

    Documentation for every game SIGGD has shipped, including codebase references, team credits, and screenshots.

    See our projects

  • Resources

    Curated lists of tools, free asset sources, external references, and recommended reading for game developers of all disciplines.

    Explore resources

  • Contribute

    Help grow this wiki. Learn how to write docs, use our templates, and follow the style guide — all you need is a GitHub account.

    How to contribute

docs.title: About SIGGD
docs.text:
SIGGD (Special Interest Group in Game Development) is a student organization at Purdue University open to everyone — whether you're a programmer, artist, designer, musician, or just someone curious about how games are made.

We meet weekly to work on game jam projects, semester-long games, and skill-building workshops. This documentation site is where we collect everything we learn along the way, so knowledge doesn't disappear when members graduate.

New to SIGGD?

Check out our About page to learn more, or jump straight into Tutorials to start learning.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
I'm new to game developmentI want to contribute to docsI want to document a project
  1. Read the Tutorials overview to find your discipline
  2. Pick an engine: start with Programming tutorials
  3. Browse Free Tools to set up your workspace
  4. Join the Discord (link in the footer) to ask questions and meet the team
  1. Read the Contributing Guide — it's a 5-minute read
  2. Review the Style Guide
  3. Pick a template and draft your page
  4. Run mkdocs serve locally to preview, then open a pull request
  1. Copy the Project Template
  2. Create a docs/projects/<your-project-name>/ folder
  3. Fill in the template — overview, codebase, team, screenshots
  4. Add your project to the nav: section of mkdocs.yml

This site is maintained by SIGGD members and deployed automatically via GitHub Actions from the SIGGD-Docs repository.

docs.title: About SIGGD
docs.text:
SIGGD (Special Interest Group in Game Development) is a student organization at Purdue University dedicated to learning and practicing game development in a collaborative, inclusive environment.

We welcome students of all skill levels and disciplines — programmers, artists, designers, audio engineers, writers, and anyone who's ever wanted to make a game.

docs.title: What We Do
docs.text:
• Weekly meetings — workshops, jam kickoffs, and project work sessions
  • Game jams — we participate in Ludum Dare, Global Game Jam, and run our own internal jams
  • Semester projects — longer-form games built collaboratively across a semester
  • Workshops — skill-building sessions on Unity, art tools, design principles, and more
  • This documentation site — so nothing we learn gets lost

docs.title: Get Involved
docs.text: Platform Link GitHub github.com/PurdueSIGGD Discord See our Linktree or ask at a meeting itch.io siggd.itch.io

docs.title: This Documentation Site
docs.text:
SIGGD Docs is built with MkDocs and the Material for MkDocs theme. It's hosted on GitHub Pages and deployed automatically when changes are merged into the main branch.

All content is written in Markdown and contributed by SIGGD members via pull requests. See the Contributing Guide to get involved.

docs.title: Contributors
docs.text:
Everyone who has contributed to SIGGD Docs is listed here. Author attribution on individual pages is handled automatically by the git-authors plugin, which tracks contributions per page via git history.

Your name will appear automatically

When you merge a pull request, your name and contribution stats are automatically shown at the bottom of every page you edited. This page serves as a high-level acknowledgment of all contributors.

docs.title: Documentation Maintainers
docs.text:
Add maintainer names and roles here as the team grows.

Name GitHub Role — — Lead Maintainer

docs.title: All Contributors
docs.text:
All contributors are credited automatically on the pages they authored. To see who wrote a specific page, scroll to the bottom — the git-authors plugin displays each contributor's name, number of lines written, and percentage of the page they authored.

You can also browse the full contribution history on GitHub.

docs.title: Contributing to SIGGD Docs
docs.text: This documentation site is built and maintained by SIGGD members — which means you can add to it. Contributions of any size are welcome: fixing a typo, adding a tutorial, or fully documenting a project you worked on.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
Prerequisites: Python 3.8+, Git, a GitHub account.

# 1. Fork the repo on GitHub, then clone your fork
git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/SIGGD-Docs.git
cd SIGGD-Docs

# 2. Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# 3. Start the local dev server with live reload
mkdocs serve

# 4. Open http://127.0.0.1:8000 in your browser

Make your changes, then:

# Validate the build before submitting (catches broken links and nav errors)
mkdocs build --strict

# Open a pull request on GitHub when ready

docs.title: What to Contribute
docs.text: Type Template to use Where to put it Tutorial Tutorial Template docs/tutorials/<category>/ Project overview Project Template docs/projects/<project-name>/ Codebase docs Codebase Template docs/projects/<project-name>/codebase/ System doc System Template docs/projects/<project-name>/codebase/systems/ Script reference Script Template docs/projects/<project-name>/codebase/scripts/ Resource page Resource Template docs/resources/ Style fix — Anywhere

docs.title: Adding Your Page to the Navigation
docs.text:
Every new page must be added to the nav: section of mkdocs.yml to appear in the sidebar. Find the section your page belongs to and add an entry:

nav:
  - Tutorials:
      - Programming:
          - tutorials/programming/index.md
          - Your New Tutorial: tutorials/programming/your-new-tutorial.md  # ← add this

If you're adding a new project, follow the pattern of example-project in the nav — create the full folder structure with codebase/, team.md, etc.

docs.title: Before You Submit a PR
docs.text:
• mkdocs build --strict passes with zero errors
  • Your page has correct frontmatter (title, description, tags)
  • You've added your page to nav: in mkdocs.yml
  • All external links you added are live
  • You followed the Style Guide

docs.title: Frontmatter Reference
docs.text:
Every page should start with YAML frontmatter between --- delimiters:

---
title: "Your Page Title"
description: "One sentence shown in search results and social previews."
tags:
  - programming   # see tags.md for the full tag list
status: draft     # draft | review | published
---

docs.title: Author Crediting
docs.text:
Author attribution is automatic — the git-authors plugin reads your git commit history and displays your name at the bottom of every page you contribute to. Just commit your changes with your real name configured in git:

git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your@email.com"

No extra steps needed. Your contribution is permanently credited.

docs.title: Markdown Cheatsheet
docs.text: A quick reference for everything you can use in SIGGD Docs pages. All examples below render correctly on this site.

docs.title: Basic Formatting
docs.text:
**bold text**
*italic text*
~~strikethrough~~
`inline code`
[Link text](https://example.com)
[Internal link](../about/index.md)

bold text · italic text · strikethrough · inline code

docs.title: Headings
docs.text:
# H1 — Page title (use once per page)
## H2 — Major section
### H3 — Sub-section
#### H4 — Rarely needed

docs.title: Lists
docs.text:
- Unordered item
- Another item
    - Nested item

1. Ordered item
2. Second item
    1. Nested ordered

- [x] Checked task
- [ ] Unchecked task
  • Checked task
  • Unchecked task

docs.title: Code Blocks
docs.text:
```csharp
public class PlayerController : MonoBehaviour
{
    public float speed = 5f;
}
```

public class PlayerController : MonoBehaviour
{
    public float speed = 5f;
}

Common language identifiers: csharp, gdscript, python, bash, yaml, json, markdown, html, css, javascript.

docs.title: Admonitions
docs.text:
!!! tip
    A helpful tip.

!!! info "Custom Title"
    Neutral information.

!!! warning
    Be careful.

!!! danger
    Serious warning.

!!! example
    A worked example.

??? note "Collapsible note"
    Click to expand.
Collapsible note

This is the collapsed content.

Tab OneTab Two

Content for tab one.

Content for tab two.

docs.title: Footnotes
docs.text:
This sentence has a footnote.[^1]

[^1]: Footnote content goes here.

docs.title: Math (LaTeX)
docs.text:
Inline: \(E = mc^2\) renders as \(E = mc^2\)

Block: 

\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}
\]

docs.title: Style Guide
docs.text: Follow these conventions when contributing to keep the site consistent and easy to read. When in doubt, match the style of existing pages.

docs.title: Voice and Tone
docs.text:
• Second person: Address the reader as "you." ("You will need to..." not "The user will need to...")
  • Active voice: "Click the button" not "The button should be clicked."
  • Direct and concise: Get to the point. Avoid padding and filler phrases.
  • Friendly, not formal: This is club documentation, not a corporate manual. Be approachable.
  • Assume good faith: Assume the reader wants to learn — don't write condescendingly.

docs.title: Headings
docs.text:
• Use sentence case for headings: "Getting started with Unity" not "Getting Started With Unity."
  • Never skip heading levels (don't go from ## to ####).
  • # (H1) is the page title — use it only once, at the top.
  • ## (H2) for major sections, ### (H3) for sub-sections.
  • Don't end headings with a period.

docs.title: Frontmatter
docs.text:
Every page must include a title and description:

---
title: "Page Title"
description: "One concise sentence describing the page's content."
tags:
  - relevant-tag
---

For tutorials, also include difficulty, time_estimate, and prerequisites.

docs.title: Code Blocks
docs.text:
Always specify the language for syntax highlighting:

```csharp
// C# code
```

```gdscript
# GDScript code
```

```bash
# Shell commands
```

Use inline code for: file paths (Assets/Scripts/), variable names (playerHealth), menu items (File > Save), and short commands (mkdocs serve).

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
• Use descriptive link text: Contributing Guide not click here.
  • For internal links, use relative paths from the current file.
  • For external links, always open in context — don't add {target="_blank"} unless the destination is a file download.

docs.title: Admonitions
docs.text:
Use admonitions to call out important information. Choose the right type:

!!! tip "Optional heading"
    A helpful tip that improves the experience but isn't required.

!!! info "Optional heading"
    Neutral supplemental information.

!!! warning "Optional heading"
    Something the reader must be aware of to avoid problems.

!!! danger "Optional heading"
    A serious warning — data loss, destructive actions, etc.

!!! example "Optional heading"
    A worked example or code sample.

Don't overuse admonitions — if every paragraph has one, none stand out.

docs.title: Images
docs.text:
• Store images in docs/assets/images/ or a project-specific subfolder.
  • Always include alt text: ![Alt text describing the image](path/to/image.png)
  • Add a caption below with italic text: *Caption explaining the image.*
  • Prefer PNG for screenshots and pixel art; JPEG for photographs.
  • Keep file sizes reasonable — compress large images before committing.

docs.title: Tables
docs.text:
Use tables for structured comparisons, not for layout. Keep them simple:

| Column A | Column B | Column C |
|----------|----------|----------|
| Value    | Value    | Value    |

docs.title: Diagrams
docs.text:
Use Mermaid diagrams for system architecture and flow charts — they render natively in the site:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start] --> B{Decision}
    B -->|Yes| C[Do thing]
    B -->|No| D[Do other thing]
```

docs.title: Difficulty Ratings (Tutorials)
docs.text: Rating Meaning beginner No prior knowledge of the topic required intermediate Comfortable with the basics, ready to go deeper advanced Significant experience with the topic expected

docs.title: Tags
docs.text:
Use lowercase, hyphen-separated tags from this list:

programming · art · game-design · audio · general · unity · godot · unreal · pixel-art · 3d · animation · ui · tools · assets · free · project · game-jam · tutorial · reference · setup · beginner · intermediate · advanced

Add new tags sparingly — check existing pages first to reuse established tags.

docs.title: Codebase Overview
docs.text: This section is the programmer reference guide for [Your Project Name]. New contributors can read this before touching source code to understand how the project is structured and where to find things.

docs.title: Repository Structure
docs.text:
Replace this with the actual folder structure using a code block:

Assets/
├── Scripts/
│   ├── Player/
│   │   ├── PlayerController.cs
│   │   └── PlayerHealth.cs
│   ├── Systems/
│   │   └── GameManager.cs
│   └── UI/
│       └── HUDController.cs
├── Prefabs/
├── Scenes/
│   └── MainScene.unity
└── Art/
    ├── Sprites/
    └── Animations/

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
Describe the high-level design approach:

  • Entry point: Which script/scene starts the game?
  • Patterns used: ECS, singleton GameManager, observer pattern, etc.
  • Key design decisions: Why was the architecture structured this way?

Add a Mermaid diagram to show the relationships between major systems:

graph TD
    GM[GameManager] --> PC[PlayerController]
    GM --> ES[EnemySpawner]
    PC --> PH[PlayerHealth]
    PH --> UI[HUDController]

docs.title: Key Systems
docs.text: System Script Description System Name SystemScript.cs Brief description Another System AnotherScript.cs Brief description

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
List any external packages or plugins the project depends on:

Package Version Purpose Unity Input System 1.7.0 New input handling TextMeshPro 3.0.6 UI text rendering

docs.title: Local Setup
docs.text:
See the Setup Guide for step-by-step instructions to get the project running locally.

Quick start:

git clone https://github.com/PurdueSIGGD/your-project.git
  1. Open in Unity Hub with [Engine Version].
  2. Open Assets/Scenes/MainScene.unity.
  3. Press Play.

docs.text:
• Setup Guide
  • Systems Documentation
  • Script Reference

docs.title: Project Info
docs.text: Field Value Engine Unity 2022.3 LTS Genre Platformer Team Size 0 Status Active Year 2024 Play itch.io Source Code GitHub

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
2–3 paragraph description of the game:

  • Paragraph 1: What is the game? What does the player do? What's the premise?
  • Paragraph 2: What was the context (game jam, semester, workshop)? What were the constraints or theme?
  • Paragraph 3: What's the team most proud of? What makes this game interesting or unique?

docs.title: Screenshots
docs.text:
Store screenshots in docs/assets/images/your-project-name/.

Caption: Main menu screen.

Caption: Gameplay in action.

docs.title: How to Play
docs.text:
List the controls clearly:

  • Move: WASD / Arrow Keys
  • Jump: Space
  • Interact: E
  • Pause: Escape

docs.title: Development Highlights
docs.text:
Note 1–3 interesting technical or design challenges the team solved. This is the most valuable content for future SIGGD members — what would you wish you had known going in?

Interesting challenge

Describe a specific problem and how you solved it. Be concrete.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Codebase Documentation
  • Setup Guide
  • Team Credits
  • Screenshots

docs.title: Resource Page Title
docs.text:
One sentence intro describing the scope of this page and who it's for.

Link freshness

External links were last verified on {{ page.meta.last_verified }}. If a link is broken, please open an issue.

docs.title: Resource Name
docs.text:
Type: Tool / Asset Pack / Tutorial / Video / Article / Book Cost: Free / Free with attribution / Free (student) / Paid ($X) Link: Resource Name

2–3 sentences describing: 1. What this resource is 2. Why it's useful for game development 3. Any caveats (license restrictions, learning curve, platform requirements, etc.)

docs.title: Another Resource
docs.text:
Type: ... Cost: ... Link: Resource Name

Description...

docs.title: Category Two
docs.text: (Copy the pattern above for each additional category.)

docs.title: Contributions Welcome
docs.text: Know a great resource that isn't listed? See the Contributing Guide or open an issue to request it.

docs.title: ScriptName.cs
docs.text:
Path: Assets/Scripts/Path/ScriptName.cs Type: MonoBehaviour / ScriptableObject / static class / etc. Namespace: YourNamespace (or "None")

One sentence summary of this script's responsibility. Be specific.

docs.title: Class Summary
docs.text: Describe the class's role in the broader codebase: what it owns, what it responds to, and what other scripts depend on it.

docs.title: Public Fields
docs.text: Field Type Default Description publicField float 1.0 What this controls aReference GameObject — What this reference is for

docs.title: Public Properties
docs.text: Property Type Get/Set Description IsActive bool Get Returns true when the script is in its active state CurrentValue float Get/Set The current value with clamping applied on set

docs.title: Serialized Private Fields
docs.text: Field Type Default Description _speed float 5.0 Internal movement speed

docs.title: void MethodName(ParameterType param)
docs.text:
What this method does.

Parameters: - param — What this parameter means.

scriptInstance.MethodName(someValue);

docs.title: ReturnType AnotherMethod()
docs.text:
What this method does and what it returns.

Returns: What the return value represents.

var result = scriptInstance.AnotherMethod();

docs.title: Unity Lifecycle Methods
docs.text: Method Usage Awake Component references resolved here Start Initial state set, events subscribed Update [Describe what runs every frame, or "Not used"] FixedUpdate [Physics updates, or "Not used"] OnDestroy Event unsubscription

docs.title: Events
docs.text: Event Type When fired OnValueChanged UnityEvent<float> When the primary value changes

docs.title: Common Use Case
docs.text:
// How another script typically interacts with this one
var script = GetComponent<ScriptName>();
script.MethodName(value);
script.OnValueChanged.AddListener(HandleValueChange);

docs.title: Called By / Calls Into
docs.text: Direction Script Why Called by GameManager.cs Initializes this script on scene load Called by UIController.cs Reads CurrentValue to update the display Calls into AudioManager.cs Triggers sound effects on state changes

docs.title: System Name
docs.text:
Script: Assets/Scripts/Path/SystemName.cs Namespace: YourNamespace (or "None") Dependencies: List components or scripts this requires

One paragraph describing what this system is responsible for and the problem it solves. Be specific — "manages player movement and animation state" is better than "controls the player."

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Describe the approach or algorithm at a conceptual level. Avoid copy-pasting code — explain the what and why, not the how.

A state diagram or flowchart is especially valuable here:

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Active : trigger condition
    Active --> Idle : reset condition
    Active --> Complete : completion condition
    Complete --> [*]

docs.title: Public Fields (Inspector-configurable)
docs.text: Field Type Default Description fieldName float 1.0 What this value controls and its effect anotherField bool true What this toggle enables someReference Transform — What this reference is used for

docs.title: ReturnType MethodName(ParameterType paramName)
docs.text:
What this method does in one sentence.

Parameters:

  • paramName — What this parameter controls.

Returns: What the return value represents.

// Example usage
var result = system.MethodName(someValue);

docs.title: void AnotherMethod()
docs.text:
Description of another public method.

system.AnotherMethod();

docs.title: Events
Configuration Tips
docs.text:
Practical tips for tuning this system's behavior:

  • Problem A? Adjust fieldName — increasing it causes X, decreasing causes Y.
  • Problem B? Check that the someReference transform is positioned correctly.

docs.title: Known Issues / Quirks
docs.text:
List any non-obvious behaviors, edge cases, or known bugs:

  • Edge case: Describe what happens in an unusual situation.
  • Known bug: Describe a known issue and any workaround.

docs.title: Called By / Calls Into
docs.text: Direction Script Why Called by ScriptName.cs Why this script calls into this system Calls into AnotherScript.cs Why this system calls into another

docs.title: Tutorial Title Here
docs.text:
What You Will Learn

By the end of this tutorial you will be able to:

  • Learning outcome one
  • Learning outcome two
  • Learning outcome three

Difficulty: Beginner · Time: ~20 minutes

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, make sure you have:

  • Unity 2022.3 LTS installed (or whichever tool this tutorial uses)
  • A basic understanding of [relevant prerequisite]

If you need help with prerequisites, see [relevant tutorial or resource link].

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: 2–3 sentences introducing the topic. Explain why this is useful for game development and what problem it solves. Avoid padding.

docs.title: Step 1: First Step Title
docs.text:
One sentence describing what this step accomplishes.

// Replace with actual code in the correct language
// Use csharp, gdscript, python, bash, etc. as the language identifier
public class ExampleClass : MonoBehaviour
{
    public float value = 1.0f;
}

Optional tip

Include a tip here only if there's something non-obvious or a common pitfall.

docs.title: Step 2: Second Step Title
docs.text:
Continue the pattern. Use screenshots or diagrams where they clarify more than words can.

Caption: brief explanation of what the screenshot is showing.

docs.title: Putting It All Together
docs.text:
Show the complete, working result after all steps are done. This gives the reader a goal to compare against.

// Full working example

Checkpoint

At this point your project should [describe the expected state]. If something looks different, check the Troubleshooting section below.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Problem OneProblem Two

Symptom: Describe exactly what the reader sees when this goes wrong.

Fix: Describe the solution clearly and completely.

Symptom: Describe exactly what the reader sees.

Fix: Describe the solution.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
After completing this tutorial, try:

  • Natural follow-on tutorial
  • Related resource

docs.title: Further Reading
docs.text:
• External resource title — one sentence on why it's useful.
  • Another resource — one sentence on why it's useful.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
Explore every game and project built by SIGGD members. Each project entry includes a playable overview, full codebase documentation, team credits, and a screenshot gallery.

The codebase docs are designed as a programmer reference guide — new members can understand a project's architecture and systems without reading all the source code.

  • Example Project

    A placeholder demonstrating the full project doc structure. Use it as a reference when documenting your own project.

    View project

Documenting a new project?

Copy the Project Template, create a folder at docs/projects/<your-project-name>/, and follow the Contributing Guide to submit a PR.

docs.title: Example Project
docs.text:
This is a template project

This page exists to demonstrate the project documentation structure. Copy the folder docs/projects/example-project/ and rename it to get started on your own project docs.

docs.title: Project Info
docs.text: Field Value Engine Unity 2022.3 LTS Genre Platformer Team Size 4 Status Archived Year 2024 Itch.io Play here Source Code GitHub

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A short paragraph (2–3 sentences) describing what the game is, the premise, and what the player does. Focus on the player experience, not technical details.

A second paragraph on what makes this game interesting or what the team was proud of. Mention any notable design or technical highlights at a high level.

docs.title: How to Play
docs.text:
• Move: WASD / Arrow Keys
  • Jump: Space
  • Interact: E
  • Pause: Escape

docs.title: Screenshots
docs.text: Add screenshots to docs/assets/images/example-project/ and link them here.

docs.title: Development Highlights
docs.text:
Brief notes on interesting problems the team solved during development. These help future members learn from this project's experience.

Interesting technical choice

Describe a specific system, mechanic, or architectural decision — and why it was made. Future members will find this more valuable than code comments.

docs.text:

To add yourself to a project's team page, edit this file and open a pull request.

docs.title: Codebase Overview
docs.text: This section is the programmer reference guide for Example Project. Use it to understand the project's architecture before reading source code, or to look up how a specific system works.

docs.title: Repository Structure
docs.text:
Assets/
├── Scripts/
│   ├── Player/
│   │   ├── PlayerController.cs
│   │   └── PlayerHealth.cs
│   ├── Enemies/
│   │   ├── EnemyAI.cs
│   │   └── EnemyHealth.cs
│   ├── Systems/
│   │   ├── GameManager.cs
│   │   └── SaveSystem.cs
│   └── UI/
│       ├── HUDController.cs
│       └── PauseMenu.cs
├── Prefabs/
├── Scenes/
│   ├── MainMenu.unity
│   └── Level01.unity
└── Art/
    ├── Sprites/
    └── Animations/

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
Describe the high-level design patterns used in this project. For example:

  • Entry point: GameManager.cs is a persistent singleton that manages scene transitions and global game state.
  • Pattern: Component-based — systems are decoupled via UnityEvents and ScriptableObject channels rather than direct references.
graph TD
    GM[GameManager] --> PS[PlayerController]
    GM --> ES[EnemySpawner]
    PS --> PH[PlayerHealth]
    ES --> EA[EnemyAI]
    PH --> UI[HUDController]

docs.title: Key Systems
docs.text: System Script Description Player Movement PlayerController.cs Handles input, physics, and animation states Player Health PlayerHealth.cs HP tracking, damage, death, and respawn Enemy AI EnemyAI.cs Patrol, detect, and chase state machine Game Manager GameManager.cs Scene management and global state Save System SaveSystem.cs JSON-based save/load to persistent data path

docs.title: Local Setup
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/PurdueSIGGD/example-project.git
  1. Open Unity Hub and add the cloned folder as a project.
  2. Open with Unity 2022.3 LTS (other versions may have import issues).
  3. Open the scene Assets/Scenes/Level01.unity.
  4. Press Play in the editor.

See Setup Guide for a detailed walkthrough including required packages.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Setup Guide — Getting the project running locally
  • Systems Documentation — How each game system works
  • Script Reference — Public API for every major script

docs.title: Local Setup Guide
docs.text: Follow these steps to get Example Project running locally for development or contribution.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Requirement Version Where to get it Unity Hub Latest unity.com/download Unity Editor 2022.3 LTS Via Unity Hub Git Any recent git-scm.com

Use the correct Unity version

Opening the project with a different Unity version may cause import errors or behavioral changes. Always use Unity 2022.3 LTS.

docs.title: Step 1: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/PurdueSIGGD/example-project.git
cd example-project

Or use GitHub Desktop: File → Clone Repository → GitHub.com → search for PurdueSIGGD/example-project.

docs.title: Step 2: Open in Unity Hub
docs.text:
1. Open Unity Hub.
  2. Click Open → Add project from disk.
  3. Navigate to the cloned folder and select it.
  4. The project will appear in Unity Hub — click it to open.

Unity will import all assets on the first open (may take a few minutes).

docs.title: Step 3: Install Required Packages
docs.text:
The project uses the following Unity packages (installed automatically via the Package Manager manifest):

Package Purpose Input System New input system for player controls TextMeshPro UI text rendering Cinemachine Camera management

If a package fails to import: Window → Package Manager → Packages: In Project and reinstall any package with an error icon.

docs.title: Step 4: Open the Main Scene
docs.text:
1. In the Project panel, navigate to Assets/Scenes/.
  2. Double-click Level01.unity to open it.
  3. Press Play (▶) in the editor toolbar.

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Pink / magenta materialsMissing script errorsInput not working in Play mode

Cause: URP (Universal Render Pipeline) shaders not compiled.

Fix: Edit → Render Pipeline → Universal Render Pipeline → Upgrade Project Materials to UniversalRP Materials.

Cause: Script compilation errors preventing Unity from loading components.

Fix: Check the Console panel for compiler errors and fix them. Usually caused by missing packages or wrong Unity version.

Cause: New Input System requires the active input handling to be set.

Fix: Edit → Project Settings → Player → Active Input Handling → Input System Package (New) — then restart the editor.

docs.title: Making Changes
docs.text:
Create a branch before making changes:

git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name

When done, push and open a pull request on GitHub.

docs.title: Script Reference
docs.text:
Quick-reference index for all major scripts. Click a script name for full API documentation.

Script Location Purpose PlayerController Scripts/Player/ Movement, input, animation PlayerHealth Scripts/Player/ HP, damage, death EnemyAI Scripts/Enemies/ Patrol, detect, chase EnemyHealth Scripts/Enemies/ Enemy HP and death GameManager Scripts/Systems/ Scene management, global state SaveSystem Scripts/Systems/ JSON save and load HUDController Scripts/UI/ Health bar, score display PauseMenu Scripts/UI/ Pause/resume, settings

Adding script docs

Copy the Script Template to create a new script reference page. Link it from the table above and add it to nav: in mkdocs.yml.

docs.title: Systems Overview
docs.text:
Each major game system has its own documentation page covering what it does, how it works, its public interface, and how to use it.

System Script Status Description Player Controller PlayerController.cs Documented Input, movement, animation Enemy AI EnemyAI.cs Needs docs Patrol, detect, chase FSM Game Manager GameManager.cs Needs docs Singleton, scene management Save System SaveSystem.cs Needs docs JSON save/load

Adding system docs

Copy the System Template and create a new file in this folder. Then add it to the table above and to the nav: in mkdocs.yml.

docs.title: Player Controller
docs.text:
Script: Assets/Scripts/Player/PlayerController.cs Namespace: None (global) Dependencies: Rigidbody2D, Animator, PlayerHealth

The PlayerController component handles all player input, movement physics, and animation state transitions. It is the primary entry point for player behavior.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The controller reads from Unity's Input System every FixedUpdate, applies forces to the Rigidbody2D, and drives the Animator via integer and bool parameters.

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Running : horizontal input
    Running --> Idle : no input
    Idle --> Jumping : jump input + grounded
    Running --> Jumping : jump input + grounded
    Jumping --> Idle : landed
    Idle --> Dead : PlayerHealth.OnDeath

Ground detection uses a small OverlapCircle cast at the groundCheck transform position each FixedUpdate.

docs.title: Public Fields
docs.text: Field Type Default Description moveSpeed float 5.0 Horizontal movement speed in units/second jumpForce float 10.0 Impulse force applied on jump groundCheck Transform — Position used for ground detection raycast groundLayer LayerMask — Layers considered "ground" coyoteTime float 0.1 Seconds the player can jump after walking off a ledge

docs.title: void DisableInput()
docs.text:
Disables all player input. Called by GameManager during cutscenes and on death.

playerController.DisableInput();

docs.title: void EnableInput()
docs.text:
Re-enables player input. Call after DisableInput() to restore control.

playerController.EnableInput();

docs.title: bool IsGrounded()
docs.text:
Returns true if the player is currently on the ground.

if (playerController.IsGrounded())
{
    // safe to spawn landing particles
}

docs.title: Events
docs.text:
Event Signature When fired OnJump UnityEvent On every jump initiation OnLand UnityEvent On landing after a jump

Subscribe in the Inspector or via code:

playerController.OnJump.AddListener(PlayJumpSound);

docs.title: Configuration Tips
docs.text:
• Feels floaty? Lower jumpForce or increase Rigidbody2D.gravityScale.
  • Feels sticky on walls? Add a zero-friction PhysicsMaterial2D to the player collider.
  • Coyote time too forgiving? Set coyoteTime to 0 to disable it.

docs.title: Called By / Calls Into
docs.text: Direction Script Why Called by GameManager DisableInput() during cutscenes Called by PlayerHealth DisableInput() on death Calls into Animator Sets Speed, IsGrounded, IsJumping parameters Calls into PlayerHealth Reads IsAlive before processing input

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
Curated resources for every discipline in game development — whether you're a programmer, artist, designer, or audio engineer.

  • Tools

    Recommended engines, editors, and productivity tools used by SIGGD members.

    View tools

  • Free Assets

    Free and open-licensed assets: sprites, 3D models, fonts, sound effects, and music.

    Find free assets

  • External References

    Tutorials, documentation, and learning resources from across the web.

    Explore references

Know a great resource?

Use the Resource Template to add it. All submissions are welcome via pull request.

docs.title: External References
docs.text: Curated external learning resources organized by discipline. These are the sites, channels, and docs that SIGGD members actually use.

docs.title: Unity Documentation
docs.text:
Link: docs.unity3d.com

The official Unity manual and scripting API. Always check the documentation version matches your Unity version.

docs.title: Godot Documentation
docs.text:
Link: docs.godotengine.org

Comprehensive and well-written official docs. The "Your first 2D game" and "Your first 3D game" tutorials are excellent starting points.

docs.title: Game Programming Patterns
docs.text:
Link: gameprogrammingpatterns.com

Free online book by Robert Nystrom. The definitive reference for common design patterns in game code (state machines, observers, object pools, etc.). Essential reading for any game programmer.

docs.title: Catlike Coding (Unity Tutorials)
docs.text:
Link: catlikecoding.com/unity/tutorials

In-depth Unity tutorials covering rendering, procedural generation, and advanced game systems. Best suited for intermediate-to-advanced programmers.

docs.title: GDC Talks (YouTube)
docs.text:
Link: youtube.com/@Gdconf

The official GDC YouTube channel. Thousands of talks from game developers on every topic. The "Vault" on gdcvault.com has even more.

docs.title: Brandon James Greer (Pixel Art)
docs.text:
Link: youtube.com/@BJGpixel

Excellent YouTube tutorials on pixel art techniques, color theory, and animation. Great for beginners and intermediate artists.

docs.title: Blender Guru
docs.text:
Link: youtube.com/@blenderguru

The classic "Donut Tutorial" and many others. Best starting point for learning Blender. Covers modeling, shading, and rendering.

docs.title: Design Doc (YouTube)
docs.text:
Link: youtube.com/@DesignDoc

Video essays analyzing game design decisions in popular games. Great for developing a vocabulary for game design thinking.

docs.title: Mark Brown / Game Maker's Toolkit
docs.text:
Link: youtube.com/@GMTK

Deep-dives into game design concepts with excellent examples. One of the best channels for game design theory.

docs.title: The Game Design Round Table (Podcast)
docs.text:
Link: thegamedesignroundtable.com

Podcast discussions between working game designers. Good for understanding how design decisions are made in practice.

docs.title: Game Audio Learning
docs.text:
Link: gameaudiolearning.com

Free resources for learning game audio implementation, FMOD, and Wwise. Covers both the technical and creative sides of game audio.

docs.title: Ask Audio — Game Audio Section
docs.text:
Link: ask.audio/articles/category/game-audio

Articles and tutorials on game audio, DAWs, and sound design for games.

docs.title: Brackeys (Unity, archived)
docs.text:
Link: youtube.com/@Brackeys

The most popular Unity tutorial channel (now archived, but still fully accessible). Excellent beginner-to-intermediate tutorials on most Unity topics.

docs.title: GDQuest (Godot)
docs.text:
Link: gdquest.com

High-quality free and paid Godot tutorials. Their free YouTube content and open-source demos are particularly valuable.

Missing a reference?

Use the Resource Template to add it. Include a one-sentence description of why it's useful.

docs.title: Free Assets
docs.text:
Curated sources for free and open-licensed game assets. Always check the specific license for each asset before using it in a public or commercial project.

Always read the license

"Free" does not always mean unrestricted. Many assets require attribution, prohibit commercial use, or require sharing derivatives under the same license. Check before you ship.

docs.title: itch.io Free Game Assets
docs.text:
Link: itch.io/game-assets/free

Thousands of free 2D asset packs from indie creators. Covers tilesets, characters, UI elements, and backgrounds. Quality varies — filter by rating to find gems.

docs.title: OpenGameArt.org
docs.text:
Link: opengameart.org

Community-driven repository of free 2D and 3D game assets. All assets are clearly labeled with CC or GPL license variants. Good for game jams.

docs.title: Kenney Assets
docs.text:
Link: kenney.nl/assets

High-quality, CC0 (public domain) game asset packs. Covers everything from UI kits to complete game asset sets. Highly recommended for prototyping.

docs.title: Poly Haven
docs.text:
Link: polyhaven.com

CC0 3D models, textures, and HDRIs. Professional quality. Excellent for environment assets and materials.

docs.title: Sketchfab (Free filter)
docs.text:
Link: sketchfab.com/features/free-3d-models

Large library with a free filter. License varies per model — check carefully. Good for finding unique characters and props.

docs.title: Google Fonts
docs.text:
Link: fonts.google.com

Open-source fonts with clean licensing (mostly OFL). Ideal for UI text. Filter by category (serif, sans-serif, monospace) and style.

docs.title: DaFont (Free for Commercial Use filter)
docs.text:
Link: dafont.com

Use the "100% Free" filter to find fonts you can use in shipped games. Great for display/title fonts with character.

docs.title: Freesound.org
docs.text:
Link: freesound.org

Community SFX library. Requires a free account. Licenses vary (CC0, CC BY, CC BY-NC) — filter by license on the search page.

docs.title: Kenney Sound Assets
docs.text:
Link: kenney.nl/assets?q=audio

CC0 sound effect packs. Well-organized by category (UI, retro, impact, etc.) Highly recommended for game jams.

docs.title: ZapSplat
docs.text:
Link: zapsplat.com

Free with a free account (requires attribution). Large library of professionally recorded SFX. Good for realistic audio.

docs.title: Free Music Archive
docs.text:
Link: freemusicarchive.org

Curated royalty-free music. Filter by license. Good variety of genres.

docs.title: OpenGameArt Music
docs.text:
Link: opengameart.org/?q=music

Game-oriented music tracks. CC licenses. Ranges from chiptune to orchestral.

Missing a source?

Know a great free asset site that isn't listed? Add it via the Resource Template.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
Recommended tools used by SIGGD members across all disciplines. Free options are listed wherever available.

Link freshness

External links were last verified on {{ page.meta.last_verified }}. If a link is broken, please open an issue.

docs.title: Unity
docs.text:
Cost: Free for personal/educational use Link: unity.com

The engine most SIGGD projects use. C# scripting, a massive asset store, and an enormous community. Best for 2D and 3D games. Requires a Unity ID.

docs.title: Godot
docs.text:
Cost: Free and open source Link: godotengine.org

A lightweight, fully open-source engine. GDScript (Python-like) or C#. Excellent for 2D games and rapidly improving for 3D. Great for beginners.

docs.title: Visual Studio Code
docs.text:
Cost: Free Link: code.visualstudio.com

Recommended code editor for most SIGGD work. Excellent extensions for C#, GDScript, Python, and Markdown. Integrates with Git natively.

docs.title: JetBrains Rider
docs.text:
Cost: Free for students (JetBrains Student License) Link: jetbrains.com/rider

Full-featured C# IDE. Significantly better Unity integration than VS Code. Apply for a free student license with your Purdue email.

docs.title: Aseprite
docs.text:
Cost: Paid ($20) — or compile from source for free Link: aseprite.org

The industry-standard pixel art editor. Excellent animation tools, palette management, and scripting support. Worth every dollar.

docs.title: Krita
docs.text:
Cost: Free and open source Link: krita.org

Professional digital painting and illustration. Great for concept art, textures, and 2D assets. Not pixel art focused, but very capable.

docs.title: Blender
docs.text:
Cost: Free and open source Link: blender.org

The gold standard in open-source 3D. Modeling, rigging, animation, rendering, and a game-ready export pipeline. Has a steep learning curve — start with beginner tutorials.

docs.title: Audacity
docs.text:
Cost: Free and open source Link: audacityteam.org

Simple audio recording and editing. Good for recording and trimming SFX. Not a full DAW, but excellent for basic needs.

docs.title: LMMS
docs.text:
Cost: Free and open source Link: lmms.io

A full DAW for music production. Good starting point for composers who don't have access to paid DAWs. Supports VST plugins.

docs.title: FMOD Studio
docs.text:
Cost: Free for indie (revenue limits apply) Link: fmod.com

Professional audio middleware widely used in the games industry. Integrates directly with Unity and Unreal. Powerful adaptive audio tools.

docs.title: Git + GitHub Desktop
docs.text:
Cost: Free Link: desktop.github.com

Git is essential for any collaborative game project. GitHub Desktop provides a user-friendly GUI for those not comfortable with the command line.

docs.title: MkDocs + Material
docs.text:
Cost: Free and open source Link: squidfunk.github.io/mkdocs-material

What this very site is built with. Markdown-based, deploys to GitHub Pages. See the Contributing Guide to add pages here.

Missing a tool?

Use the Resource Template to add it via pull request.

docs.title: Tutorials & Guides
docs.text:
Browse tutorials by category. Each tutorial includes a difficulty rating and estimated time to complete. All experience levels are welcome.

  • Programming

    Unity, Godot, C#, GDScript, game systems architecture, and tooling.

    Browse

  • Art & Assets

    Pixel art, 3D modeling, animation, UI design, and asset pipelines.

    Browse

  • Game Design

    Mechanics design, level design, player psychology, and writing GDDs.

    Browse

  • Audio

    Sound effects, music composition, FMOD integration, and audio implementation.

    Browse

  • General

    Git workflow, project management, tooling setup, and collaborative skills.

    Browse

Want to write a tutorial?

Use the Tutorial Template and follow the Contributing Guide. Your knowledge is valuable — share it!

docs.title: Art & Asset Tutorials
docs.text: Tutorials for artists and asset creators at every skill level — whether you're drawing your first sprite or rigging a character in Blender. This section covers the full pipeline: creating assets and getting them into your engine.

docs.title: Topics Covered Here
docs.text:
This section is home to tutorials on subjects like:

  • Pixel art — Aseprite basics, color palettes, shading techniques, animation frames
  • Sprite workflow — sprite sheets, slicing in Unity/Godot, pivot points, atlases
  • Character animation — walk cycles, attack animations, squash & stretch, easing
  • Tilemaps — creating tileset graphics, autotiling rules, Unity Tilemap / Godot TileSet
  • UI/UX design — button states, layout grids, readable fonts, consistent iconography
  • 3D modeling — Blender fundamentals, low-poly techniques, UV unwrapping, export to FBX/GLTF
  • Texturing — texture painting, PBR materials, normal maps, sprite-based materials
  • Visual effects — particle systems in Unity/Godot, 2D lighting, screen effects
  • Asset pipelines — import settings, compression, atlas packing, addressables
  • Tools — Aseprite, Krita, Blender, Photoshop alternatives, asset converters

No tutorials yet — be the first!

Use the Tutorial Template to share your art workflow. A quick "how I set up my Aseprite color palette" or "how to export Blender models for Unity" is exactly what newer artists need.

See the Contributing Guide for how to submit.

docs.title: Audio Tutorials
docs.text: Game audio is one of the most underrated ways to make a game feel polished. This section covers the full pipeline from creating audio to implementing it in your engine, including middleware like FMOD.

docs.title: Topics Covered Here
docs.text:
This section is home to tutorials on subjects like:

  • Sound effects — recording, synthesis, editing in Audacity, quick SFX with Bfxr/ChipTone
  • Music composition — writing game music in a DAW, looping cleanly, genre-appropriate styles
  • DAW basics — LMMS, GarageBand, or any other DAW: tracks, MIDI, mixing, exporting
  • Audio in Unity — AudioSource/AudioClip, AudioMixer, spatial 3D audio, 2D UI sounds
  • Audio in Godot — AudioStreamPlayer, AudioBus, positional audio
  • FMOD with Unity — setup, events, parameters, adaptive/dynamic audio
  • Adaptive audio — music that reacts to gameplay state, layered stems, dynamic mixing
  • Audio polish — reverb and effects, ducking, footsteps, impact weight
  • Performance — audio compression, streaming vs. preloaded, memory considerations
  • Voice over — recording dialogue, noise reduction, implementation patterns

No tutorials yet — be the first!

Use the Tutorial Template to document your audio workflow. Even a short "how to export clean audio from Audacity for Unity" saves the next person an hour of searching.

See the Contributing Guide for how to submit.

docs.title: Game Design Tutorials
docs.text: The craft of making games fun — from high-level theory to practical tools you can apply in a game jam this weekend. Game design is for everyone on the team, not just dedicated designers.

docs.title: Topics Covered Here
docs.text:
This section is home to tutorials on subjects like:

  • Core game loops — primary loops, secondary loops, feedback cycles, player motivation
  • Level design — flow, pacing, spatial storytelling, difficulty curves, tutorialization
  • Writing a GDD — what to include, what to skip, keeping it collaborative and lean
  • Mechanics design — prototyping an idea, verbs and nouns, emergence and depth
  • Playtesting — how to run a playtest session, what to observe vs. what to ask
  • Difficulty & balancing — skill curves, rubber-banding, accessibility options
  • Narrative design — environmental storytelling, dialogue trees, ludonarrative coherence
  • UI/UX principles — affordances, feedback, information hierarchy in game UI
  • Prototyping — paper prototypes, grey-box levels, "is this fun in 20 minutes?"
  • Game jam strategy — scoping for 48 hours, cutting features, shipping something playable
  • Postmortems — how to run a postmortem, what went well, what to improve

No tutorials yet — be the first!

Use the Tutorial Template to write about a design lesson from a project you've worked on. Concrete examples from real SIGGD games are the most valuable thing we can document.

See the Contributing Guide for how to submit.

docs.title: General Tutorials
docs.text: Skills that every team member benefits from, regardless of discipline — the collaborative and organizational glue that makes game jams and semester projects run smoothly.

docs.title: Topics Covered Here
docs.text:
This section is home to tutorials on subjects like:

  • Git basics — cloning, committing, pushing, pulling, resolving merge conflicts
  • Git for game jams — branching strategy, handling large binary files with Git LFS
  • GitHub workflow — pull requests, code review, issues, project boards
  • Setting up your environment — Unity Hub, VS Code + extensions, Godot, Git, GitHub Desktop
  • Project scoping — estimating what's actually achievable in a jam or a semester
  • Team roles — how to split work across programmers, artists, designers, and audio
  • Communication — how to give and receive design feedback, async vs. sync collaboration
  • Postmortems — why to do them, how to run them, what makes a good one
  • Playtesting sessions — running a playtest, note-taking, synthesizing feedback
  • Game jam strategy — Ludum Dare, Global Game Jam, how to prepare and what to expect
  • Open source contributing — forks, PRs, reviewing other people's docs changes

No tutorials yet — be the first!

Use the Tutorial Template to write up a workflow tip you wish you'd known earlier. The Git and setup category is especially valuable for new members joining mid-year.

See the Contributing Guide for how to submit.

docs.title: Programming Tutorials
docs.text: Everything you need to write game code — from setting up your first Unity project to architecting complex game systems. Tutorials here cover both Unity (C#) and Godot (GDScript/C#), with engine-agnostic programming concepts mixed in.

docs.title: Topics Covered Here
docs.text:
This section is home to tutorials on subjects like:

  • Getting started — Unity setup, Godot setup, first scripts, project structure
  • Core scripting — C# and GDScript fundamentals, MonoBehaviour lifecycle, Nodes
  • Player systems — movement, input handling, cameras, physics, animation state machines
  • Game systems — health, inventory, save/load, scene management, game managers
  • Enemy & AI — patrol, detection, chase, pathfinding with NavMesh / NavigationAgent
  • UI systems — HUDs, menus, pause screens, ScriptableObject data binding
  • Shaders — shader basics in HLSL / ShaderGraph / Godot's visual shader editor
  • Patterns — Singleton, Observer, State Machine, Object Pooling, ECS concepts
  • Procedural generation — tilemaps, dungeons, noise-based terrain
  • Performance — profiling, batching, draw calls, garbage collection

No tutorials yet — be the first!

Use the Tutorial Template to write up something you know. Even a short "how to set up the Input System in Unity 2022" is genuinely useful for newer members.

See the Contributing Guide for how to submit.
